coredata.dc:title: HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA as carries of gambogic acid for inhibiting VX2 tumor cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yubing
Liu, Wenwen
Liu, Xiangnong
Yang, Yuxiang
coredata.dc:description: Ellipsoidal hollow magnetic nanoparticles (HMNPs) were first synthesized through a hydrothermal method and coated with SiO2 via the Stöber method, followed by template hydrothermal synthesis using C18TMS/TEOS volume ratio for silica-shell formation. Then, the core–shell structure was grafted with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), dopamine hydrochloride (PDA), and folic acid (FA) by amide reaction to obtain HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA. Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and N2 adsorption/desorption analysis confirmed the successful construction of the HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA nanoscale carrier–cargo composite. The carrier HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA had improved loading (51.63 %) and higher release (47.18 %) of gambogic acid (GA) at an acidic pH (5.7) than the release (32.23 %) at an acidic pH (7.4), showing the pH response of PDA to the surrounding environment of tumor cells. Cytotoxicity test results showed that the cell survival rate was above 95 %, indicating that the carrier–cargo had low cytotoxicity. Cell lethality was greatly improved after loading GA, and the advantages of using FA-grafted HMNPs as a targeting ligand for tumor imaging were demonstrated by SPECT imaging analysis. These results showed that HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA had good biocompatibility and could be used for the treatment of VX2 tumors after loading GA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
FA
Targeting
Cytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of hollow spherical Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 nanoparticles
Determination of kinetic models of drug release
VX2 targeting ability in pulmonary vascular bed and liver

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM of (a) Fe2O3@SiO2, upper-right corner: α-Fe2O3 and (b) Fe2O3@SiO2@mSiO2-C18, top left: HRTEM (c)HRTEM of mSiO2, and (d) TEM of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA, upper-right corner: several separate clear HRTEM images.
Ftir of HMNPs, HMNPs-NH2, and HMNPs-GQDs (a); HMNPs@PDA, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA(b).

(a) XPS spectrum of HMNPs-GQDs、HMNPs@PDA, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs; (b) C1s peaks of HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs; (c) C1s peaks of HMNPs-GQDs; and (d) N1s peaks of HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs
(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs, HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA; (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm; (c) pore-size distribution; and (d) magnetic hysteresis loops.

(a) Higuchi kinetic curve, (b) first-order kinetic curve, and (c) Weibull kinetic curve; (d) sustained release rate of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA to GA at different pH; release mechanism at pH (e) 5.7 and (f) 7.4.

(a) and (b) MTT cytotoxicity assay results of VX2 cells treated with HMNPs-GQDs (GA), HMNPs@PDA-GQDs (GA), and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA (GA) at different concentrations for 24 h; (c) and (d) MTT cytotoxicity assay results of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA(GA) on MCF-7 and VX2 cells.
(a) HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and (b) HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA laser confocal fluorescence images of VX2 cells.
(a) Tumor volume growth curves of VX2 tumors after various treatments, and (b) transplanted VX2 tumor cells.
CT images of (a) 99TcmO4 -, (b) HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-99Tcm, and (c) HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA-99Tcm

head.ce:title: HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA as carries of gambogic acid for inhibiting VX2 tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liuzhou Institute of Technology, Guangxi 545616, Liuzhou, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liuzhou Institute of Technology, Guangxi 545616, Liuzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai, 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis Test Center, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225009, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis Test Center, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ellipsoidal hollow magnetic nanoparticles (HMNPs) were first synthesized through a hydrothermal method and coated with SiO2 via the Stöber method, followed by template hydrothermal synthesis using C18TMS/TEOS volume ratio for silica-shell formation. Then, the core–shell structure was grafted with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), dopamine hydrochloride (PDA), and folic acid (FA) by amide reaction to obtain HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA. Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and N2 adsorption/desorption analysis confirmed the successful construction of the HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA nanoscale carrier–cargo composite. The carrier HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA had improved loading (51.63 %) and higher release (47.18 %) of gambogic acid (GA) at an acidic pH (5.7) than the release (32.23 %) at an acidic pH (7.4), showing the pH response of PDA to the surrounding environment of tumor cells. Cytotoxicity test results showed that the cell survival rate was above 95 %, indicating that the carrier–cargo had low cytotoxicity. Cell lethality was greatly improved after loading GA, and the advantages of using FA-grafted HMNPs as a targeting ligand for tumor imaging were demonstrated by SPECT imaging analysis. These results showed that HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA had good biocompatibility and could be used for the treatment of VX2 tumors after loading GA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy [1–3], radiotherapy [4–6], and surgical treatment [7] are widely used as cancer treatments. However, these approaches lead to varying degrees of damage to physiological systems. Therefore, developing carrier systems for drug transport and delivery is important for targeted cancer therapy.

In 1979, Couvreur et al. [8] synthesized polyalkyl cyanoacrylate nanoparticles for drug loading and transport. This novel intracellular drug delivery system was ultrafine and metabolizable; however, it could associate with various drugs in a non-specific manner and be degraded in vitro. The most critical applications of magnetic nano iron oxide and magnetic mesoporous materials are targeted drug delivery for therapeutic purposes [9], among them, magnetic nanoparticles inside mesoporous silica (mSiO2) have a large specific surface area, pore size, and volume; thus, they are characterized by ease of surface modification through alkoxysilane chemistry. Therefore, mSiO2 is an ideal candidate for smart nano-DDS, allowing high loading efficiency of payloads. Avedian et al. [10] prepared a tumor-targeting drug nanocarrier based on magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSNs) with Fe3O4 as the core and polyimide-conjugated folic acid as the pH-responsive shell. Then, erlotinib was loaded as an anticancer drug, and the release characteristics were evaluated at pH 5.5 and 7.4 within 4 days. The release of erlotinib increased to 63 % at lower pH, compared to 33 % at normal pH. Moreover, erlotinib-loaded MMSNs inhibited the proliferation of HeLa cells. Magnetic MCM-41 loaded with the antimalarial drug chloroquine was evaluated in vitro as a drug delivery system by de Andrade [11], showing negligible cytotoxic effects on various cell lines. Well-dispersed drug molecules on the pore walls of mesoporous silica facilitated the diffusion of the liquid medium into the pores to dissolve and release the drug from the nanocarrier, which had the potential to be applied for poorly soluble drugs, such as gambogic acid (GA), controlling the release of chemotherapy drugs, thereby increasing the therapeutic efficacy of the targeted drugs. With Fe compounds in the core, a mesoporous silica shell can be used as a pH- and temperature-stimuli-responsive drug carrier to deliver anticancer drugs for cancer therapy [12,13] owing to their distinct surface chemistry, good biocompatibility, and inducible magnetic moments. Thus, magnetic mesoporous materials have many advantages as a drug delivery system.

The folate receptor (FR), which is widely distributed in colon carcinomas, is a potentially useful target for cancer therapy and imaging. Therefore, the covalent conjugation of folic acid (FA) to drug carriers could attain selective targeting to the tumor mass [14]. Ghosh et al. [15] prepared FA-conjugated polyacrylic-acid-capped mesoporous silica nanoparticles (∼47.6 nm in diameter) for pH-dependent targeted delivery of chrysin to breast cancer (MCF-7) cells, as FA exhibited higher affinity for FRs. FRs are overexpressed on various cancers, including breast, ovarian, lung, and kidney cancers and those derived from epithelial tissues. Such affinity enables folate-tagged nanocarriers to selectively target cancer cells. After the attachment of folate to FRs within caveolae, folate-tagged nanocarriers are internalized via the endocytic pathway.

GA, a compound that has long been used in Chinese herbal medicine, has low toxicity. Tumor cells do not easily develop resistance to GA, and GA leaves behind little residue in the body after treatment. The in vitro and in vivo anticancer activity of GA is mainly attributed to its down regulation of telomerase activity, thus activating the damaged apoptotic pathway in cancer cells [16–18], which can inhibit the growth of liver, lung, gastric, and breast cancer cells. Guo et al. [19] studied the inhibitory effect of fusiformic acid on hepatoma cells (SMMC-7721), confirming obvious cytotoxicity at a concentration of 0.625–5.0 mg/L. At 2 mg/L, fusiformic acid changed the morphology of SMMC-7721 cells significantly and inhibited their telomerase activity. Bishayee et al. [20] investigated the treatment of CD71 and MYCN cells in neuroblastoma using liana. GA preferentially reduced the growth of neuroblastoma cells compared with that of non-cancer cells. It can target CD71, leading to changes in CD71 expression and CD71-dependent death of neuroblastoma cells. The growth inhibition of MYCN cells was achieved by the downregulation of MYCN expression by histone deacetylase inhibitor (HDACI), thus reducing the survival rate of neuroblastoma cells. Therefore, the clinical application of GA can be further studied in terms of anticancer activity, drug delivery system, and combined therapy [21].

In this study, difunctional pH-responsive HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA mesoporous nanocomposites were employed as carriers for anticancer drugs. The system showed excellent drug loading, sustained release, and low cytotoxicity. FA conjugation enhanced the targeting capability of the composite nanoparticles. After GA loading, the carrier system exhibited excellent targeted anti-tumor effects.

Citric acid, naphthenic acid, isooctyl alcohol, ferric sulfate, polyoxyethylene (5) nonylphenyl ether (Igepal CO-520), tetraethyl silicate (TEOS), octadecyl trimethoxysilane (C18TMS), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), EDC, NHS, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and FA were purchased from National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The other reagents used were GA (Nanjing Jingzhu Biotechnology Co., Ltd.), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; Wuhan Dr. Bioengineering Co., Ltd.), MTT (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), RPMI-1640 medium (Invitrogen, USA), embryonic bovine serum (Hyclone, USA), and sodium perrhenate injection (customized by Changhai Hospital). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

A Tecnai-12 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 120 kV was used to characterize the particle morphology and size. The hydration diameter of the nanoparticles was measured using a laser-scattering particle size analyzer. D/max 2550 VB/PC type X-ray diffractometer (Kα line of copper target, λ = 0.15406 nm) was used for measurement at room temperature. Magna-IR 550 infrared spectrometer was used for collecting FTIR spectra. VSM BHS-55 vibrating sample magnetometer was used to determine the magnetic properties of the sample. Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer was used to determine the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra.

Citric acid (2 g) was weighed and pyrolyze at 200 °C in a clean crucible on LC-DB-1AB type thermostatic heating plate (Shanghai Lichen Instrument Technology Co., LTD). When the white crystals changed to an orange liquid, the crucible was removed and cooled to 25–30 °C. Then, deionized water was added to the mixture and stirred. The obtained liquid was placed in a pre-prepared dialysis bag (interception molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the dialyzed liquid was subjected to freeze-drying to obtain a yellowish solid powder. GQDs in aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL) were prepared for subsequent use.

Briefly, 20 mL of naphthenic acid, 10 mL of isooctyl alcohol, and 10 mL of sulfonated kerosene were placed in a round-bottom flask, of which a mixed solution of ammonia water and deionized water (volume ratio: 1:1) was added while stirring for saponification, then, 20 mL of iron sulfate solution was added to the above solution, followed by stirring at room temperature for 1 h. After the reaction, the solution was separated and washed to retain the organic phase. The organic phase was added to the PTFE autoclave, and deionized water was added (the volume ratio of the aqueous and organic phases was maintained at 1:3). The sealed autoclave was heated in an oven and stirred for 2 h, and the temperature was maintained at 180 °C. Finally, the products in the reactor were centrifuged and washed with anhydrous ethanol three times. The obtained solids were vacuum-dried to obtain a reddish-brown solid product.

Igepal CO-520 (0.5 g) was added to cyclohexane (11 mL) and dispersed evenly using an ultrasonic oscillator. Dry α-Fe2O3 (1.25 mg) and ammonia in water solution (volume ratio: 1:1) (0.2 mL) were added to the mixture under stirring. Finally, TEOS (0.28 mL) was added slowly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hollow spherical Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Boiled and cooled deionized water (2 mL), ethanol (10 mL), and ammonia (0.72 mL) were added to the flask with 5 mL α-Fe2O3@SiO2 (0.05g) anhydrous ethanol solution. Then, 0.3 mL of C18TMS/TEOS solution with an unchanged volume ratio of 1:2.47 was added slowly. The reaction of the mixed solution was carried out at 25 °C for 6 h, and α-Fe2O3@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 was obtained by centrifugation.

α-Fe2O3@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 (0.05 g) was added to the mixture of 28 mL of boiled and cooled deionized water and 2 mL anhydrous ethanol and dispersed evenly with an ultrasonic oscillator. The reaction was carried out at 130 °C for 20 h in a high-pressure reactor of PTFE to change the polysiloxane with the α-Fe2O3@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 shell structure from a low to a high polystate. The product was separated by centrifugation, vacuum-dried for 6 h, and calcined at 550 °C for 6 h to form an outer pore under N2 atmosphere. Then, hydrogen and nitrogen (H2(4 %)/N2) were inleted simultaneously and calcined at 400 °C for 3 h to obtain magnetic nano-hollow spheres Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 (HMNPs).

HMNPs (100 mg), anhydrous toluene (50 mL), and APTES (1 mL) were mixed evenly in a round bottom flask, mechanically stirred, and refluxed at 110 °C for 12 h in an oil bath. The product was cooled to room temperature, separated by permanent magnet, washed three times with anhydrous ethanol, and subjected to vacuum-drying in an oven for 6 h. Then, aminoized HMNPs were obtained. The GQD solution with different concentrations was diluted to 20 mL, and EDC (10 mg) and NHS (8 mg) were added. The solution was mixed at 37 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, aminoated HMNPs (50 mg) were added to oscillate at 30 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the target product, HMNPs-GQDs, was obtained by magnetic separation and lyophilization for 24 h.

HMNPs (20 mg) were dispersed in 50 mL Tris buffer solution (∼10 mM at pH 8.5) via ultrasonic treatment, and dopamine hydrochloric acid solution was slowly dropped into the mixture while stirring. The mixture was centrifuged and the precipitate was washed after 6 h of reaction, and the HMNPs@PDA was obtained after freeze-drying for 24 h. GQD solution (0.1 mg/ml, 5 mL) was diluted to 20 mL with deionized water and oscillated at 37 °C for 2 h after adding EDC (10 mg) and NHS (8 mg). HMNPs@PDA (20 mg) was added and reacted for 2 h to graft GQDs onto the HMNPs@PDA surface. FA (50 mg) and NHS (80 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL MES buffer solution and stirred for 30 min, and the black solid was obtained by magnetic separation. The sample was redispersed in PBS, stirred overnight in the dark, separated magnetically, and lyophilized for 24 h to obtain HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA.

GA (0.04 g) was dissolved in DMSO in a 100-mL volumetric flask. 20 mL, 10 mL, 6 mL, 5 mL, 4 mL, 3 mL, 2 mL, and 1.25 mL of the above solution were made up to volume with 20 mL to prepare GA solutions of different concentrations within the range 25∼400 μg/ml 10 mg HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA was dispersed uniformly by ultrasound in 20 mL inactivated fetal bovine serum RPMI-1640 culture medium, and 20 mL of the prepared GA solutions at different concentrations was added and shaken at 4 °C for 48 h. The product was separated by centrifuge, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured.

HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA (10 mg) loaded with the drug was dispersed in 25 mL phosphoric acid buffer solution (at pH 5.7 and 7.4) by ultrasound and shaken continuously at 37 °C. Absorbance was measured at set intervals after centrifugation, and the same pH value of phosphoric acid buffer solution was added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of kinetic models of drug release
ce:section.ce:para: Three kinetic models of drug release were used to study the release kinetics mechanism of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA in vitro. Fitting was determined according to the R2 of the fitting correlation coefficient.

First was the Higuchi model:
Q t
=
K H
t
1 2
, where Qt is the cumulative release percentage of the drug at time t and KH is the diffusion constant [22].

The second was a first-order kinetic model:
Q
t
=
Q m
[
1 − exp
(
− k t
)
]
, where Qt is the cumulative drug release at time t, Qm is the cumulative drug release at equilibrium, and K is the first-order kinetic release constant [23].

The third was the Weibull dynamics model:
Q
t
=
Q m
[
1 − exp
(
− a
t b
)
]
, where Qt is the cumulative release of drugs at time t, Qm is the cumulative drug release at equilibrium, and a and b are constants [24].

MTT assays were performed. The VX2 cells in good condition were evenly spread into 96-well plate and cultured in 5 % CO2 at 37 °C incubator. When the cells attached to the wall and the density was approximately 80 %, the supernatant was discarded. HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA (concentration per sample were 0, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 125 μg/mL) were incubated with VX2 cells for 24 h in the same incubator conditions. After incubation, the remaining solution in each well was collected and rinsed with PBS. The background was measured using an enzyme marker, and the MTT solution was added to the well plates. After incubation for 4 h, the MTT residue was removed, and DMSO was added to dissolve the crystallization. Finally, absorbance values were measured by an enzyme-labeling at 570 nm, and the cell survival rate was calculated as follows:
Cell
viability
( % )
=
O D
e x p e r i m e n t a l
−
O D
b l a n k
O D
t u m o r
−
O D
b l a n k
× 100 %
where ODexperimental represents the absorbance value of the sample, ODblank represents the absorbance value of the culture medium and MTT, and ODtumor represents the absorbance value of the cells.

The MCF-7 and VX2 cells were plated evenly into a 96-well plate and cultured at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 atmosphere. When cells grew at approximately 80 % density, the remaining culture medium was aspirated. Then, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA were added, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 20 min, cellular uptake for 2 h. Nuclei were stained with 1 μg/mL DAPI for 20 min. For each substance added, the residual solution was discarded and washed with PBS 2–3 times. Finally, a drop of anti-fluorescence quencher was added, and cells were observed under laser confocal microscope.

Free Na99TcmO4, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-99Tcm was carried out with the same radioactive dose of Na99TcmO4 eluent, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-99Tcm and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA-99Tcm (∼10–20 mg). In the VX2 tumor magnetic targeting intervention experiment, approximately 37 MBq (∼1 mCi) of the labeled compound (HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA-99Tcm) was directly injected into the VX2 tumor in the lung, and SPECT imaging analysis was performed after 1 h.

As shown in the upper-right corner of Fig. 1 a, α-Fe2O3 microspheres with a length of approximately 80 nm and a width of 40 nm were smooth and uniformly dispersed. The silica-coating shell was ∼6 nm (Fig. 1a). The product Fe2O3@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 was obtained by re-coating with C18TMS and TEOS, as shown in top left of Fig. 1b. The thickness of the shell was ∼18 nm, and the cavity gap between the core and the shell was ∼2.5 nm. After calcination in H2 and N2, there was no significant change in the particle size, and it remained in the range of 120 nm and 150 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b. Fig. 1 (c) shows an HRTEM image of the SiO2 shell, which has a regular arrangement of parallel channels. The pore center spacing was ∼2.96 nm, corresponding to the mesoporous SiO2 synthesized using octadecyl trimethoxysilane C18TMS as the template, as reported in the literature [25]. The characterization results of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA are shown in Fig. 1 (d). Bright white GQDs with a diameter range of 6–10 nm aggregated on the surface of an elliptical mesoporous hollow sphere sized 120–150 nm (upper-right corner of Fig. 1 (d)).

To determine the molecular structures and chemical bonds in HMNPs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the peak at 582 cm−1 was characteristic of Fe–O bonds, confirming the existence of the Fe3O4 molecule in the HMNPs. The wide band at 3450 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching vibration. Two Si–O–Si-related bands were observed at 1105 cm−1 and 456 cm−1, respectively, confirming the existence of SiO2 shells on the surface of HMNPs. The stretching vibration of –CH at 2933 cm−1 and the peak at 1480 cm−1 characteristic of hydrogen on the amino group were present [26], representing the conjugation of APTES on the surface of HMNPs.

After covalent binding with GQDs, new peaks at 1394 cm−1 were noted, which can be attributed to the C–N stretching of the EDC/NHS. The other peak at 1584 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of NH–CO-, indicating the formation of an amide bond in the HMNPs-GQDs. Fig. 2b shows peaks at 1086 cm−1 and 967 cm−1, which are attributed to the Si–O–Si stretching vibration and silanol vibration, respectively. After encapsulation with PDA, the peak at 1636 cm−1 was strengthened, attributed to the aromatic ring framework stretching vibration. Another characteristic peak of PDA was weakened at 1350 cm−1, confirming the presence of the PDA coating on the surface of HMNPs. The broad absorption peak at 3438 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H. Regarding HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA, a new peak at 1504 cm−1 and the C=O stretching vibration peak of ketone or aldehyde at 1720 cm−1 indicate the grafting of the targeted ligand FA onto the composite nanoparticles.

XPS analysis results are shown in Fig. 3 a. All three composite nanoparticles have Si, C, N, and O elements. The intensity of the Si2p peak in the XPS spectrum of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA was slightly less than that in the spectrum of HMNPs@PDA, indicating the successful incorporation of FA. The main peak at 285 eV is attributed to the C–C or C–H bond (Fig. 3b). The C1s peak was further divided (Fig. 3c), showing that the carbon atoms in the HMNPs-GQDs mainly exist in the form of C–C peak at 285 eV, C–O peak at 286.6 eV, and O=C–N peak at 288.7 eV. In Fig. 3d, relative to the XPS spectrum of HMNPs-GQDs, in those of HMNPs@PDA and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA, the intensity of the N1s peak at 406 eV increased, indicating the presence of the PDA layer.

The fluorescence spectra of HMNPs, HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA measured at 350 nm are shown in Fig. 4 a. Those of GQD aqueous solution under two different light sources are shown in the upper-right corner of Fig. 4a. Under sunlight, GQD aqueous solution showed yellow fluorescence, whereas under UV lamp, it showed green fluorescence. GQDs and HMNPs-GQDs both had absorption peaks at 396 nm, but HMNPs-GQDs had weaker fluorescence intensity. Covalent bonds are formed between the amino group on HMNPs and the carboxyl group on GQDs, resulting in the transfer of electrons and energy and a certain degree of fluorescence quenching of quantum dots. The peaks of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA were also at the same position, and the fluorescence intensity did not decrease significant after grafting FA. These results indicate that the composite nanoparticle HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA were successfully prepared and could be used for cell-labeled fluorescence imaging and live tracking cellular dynamic processes.

The N2 sorption isotherms of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA belong to typical type IV adsorption at a relative pressure range of 0.01–0.99, as shown in Fig. 4b. The N2 sorption amount slowly increased with increasing p/p0, and the adsorption isotherm curved upward in the high-pressure region, leading to the formation of adsorption hysteresis loops belonging to the H4 type isotherms in the IUPAC.

The pore sizes of the composite nanoparticles were mainly distributed in the 2.53–5.81 nm range, as shown in Fig. 4c, with a mode of 2.53 nm. The specific surface area and pore volume measurements are listed in Table 1 . From the above data, the prepared composite nanoparticles HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA can be used for drug loading.

The magnetic hysteresis loops of HMNPs, HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA were determined at room temperature (Fig. 4d), exhibiting S-type hysteresis and relatively small coercivity. The saturation magnetization Ms of the HMNPs-GQDs was 26.81 emu/g, less than that of the HMNPs, while residual magnetization of the former increased to ∼3.84 emu/g, higher than that of the latter's, indicating that GQDs were successfully grafted onto HMNPs surface.

The hysteresis loop of the HMNPs@PDA-GQDs was S type, the coercivity was relatively small, the saturation magnetization Ms was 17.07 emu/g, and the residual magnetization was ∼4.47 emu/g. After grating FA, the saturation magnetization Ms decreased to 15.06 emu/g, and the residual magnetization increased to 5.32 emu/g. Thus, grafting functional groups have negligible influence on the magnetic properties of raw materials.

To study the effect of surface modification and loaded drugs on the surface charge of the composite mesoporous particles, zeta potential was measured at pH 7.4 (Fig. S1). The zeta potential of HMNPs-NH2 was positively charged (21.56 mV) owing to the amino group on the surface, while that of HMNPs-GQDs (18.80 mV) was less positive because of the negative charge of the carboxyl group. That of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs (20.20 mV) was more positive because of the amino group on the PDA. However, those of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA (GA) were reduced to 10.1 mV and −7.00 mV, respectively, because of the presence of carboxyl groups. Overall, the positively charged HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA particles are more likely to penetrate the anionic cell membrane, exhibiting good cell penetration and representing potential as drug carriers.

The highest loading and embedding efficiency of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA reached 11.74 % and 51.63 % (Table 2 ). When the GA dosage exceeded 4 mg, the loading amount began to become saturated. The adsorption capacity of mesoporous hollow spheres with a certain surface area and pore volume is limited. Thus, when the surface adsorption layer reaches saturation, the excess drug molecules can only pile up to the second layer of the surface, preventing drug loading. Therefore, the most suitable dosage of GA was 4 mg, the drug encapsulation rate was 45.34 %, and the drug loading was 8.31 %.

The three kinetic models are shown in Fig. 5 . Drug release from the HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA does not conform to the Higuchi kinetic model (Fig. 5a), with a fitting correlation coefficient of 0.7308. This indicates an underestimation of the time of rapid release of the drug, implying that drug release is not completely controlled by the diffusion process. Fitting with the first-order kinetic model, a correlation coefficient of 0.8263 was also obtained (Fig. 5b). So, if we use a first-order kinetic model, we should underestimate the rapid release point of GA and overestimate the stable release of GA. Due to these facts, we had to use the Weibull kinetic model instead, a correlation coefficient of 0.9825 was obtained (Fig. 5c), indicating that sustained release in HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA agrees well with the Weibull kinetic model. According to this model, the dosage of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA nanoparticles to tumor cells can be feasibly designed.

Drug release is divided into two stages. The initial release involves the diffusion of drug from the outer surface of the nanomaterials, whereas the latter stage involves the slow diffusion of drug molecules from the hollow spheres through the mesopores into the external environment. Owing to the resistance due to varying pore sizes and the pressure difference between the inside and outside of the hollow sphere, the drug diffuses slowly, reaching equilibrium. The release rate of drug molecules is, thus, determined by the slower step.

We also explored the pH dependence of sustained release of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA at pH 7.4 and 5.7. At pH 7.4, the cumulative release of GA by HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA was 33.39 % and 32.23 %, respectively. At pH 5.7, they increased to 50.13 % and 47.18 % (Fig. 5d), respectively, indicating that the PDA film encapsulated on the HMNP surface is a pH-dependent membrane that acts as a control switch for drug release.

This can be explained by the hydrogen bond between HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA and GA. The former contains N–H, O–H, and C=O groups, but the latter contains hydroxyl, carboxyl, and C=O groups, suggesting that there should be a hydrogen bond between them. The dependence of release rate on pH may be attributed to the inhibition effect of the hydrogen bond between HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA and GA on the release of the latter from mesoporous particles.

Bhaskar et al. [27] developed a simple procedure to establish whether diffusion is the rate-limiting step. For a particle diffusion-controlled release, they [27] derived the following equation:
ln
(
1 −
x t
)
= − 1.59
(
6 /
d p
)
1.3
D 0.65
t 0.65
, where dp is the diameter of the nanoparticles and D is the diffusivity. This suggests that particle diffusion control can be determined by simply testing for the linearity between
− log
(
1 − X t
)
and t0.65. After application to the experimental data, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9872) was obtained for release at pH 5.7 (Fig. 5e), confirming that the diffusion through the particle is the rate-limiting step [28].

By contrast, the relationship between −log(1-xt) and t0.65 was nonlinear at pH 7.4 (Fig. 5f). This may be explained as follows. Under acidic conditions, the drug molecules adsorbed on the hollow sphere diffuse outwards, while the internal drug molecules diffuse outwards because the hollow sphere has the same charge as itself. Thus, these two repel each other. Under neutral pH, the drug molecules loaded on the surface of the nanocarrier are rapidly released, whereas those in the cavity exist stably and diffuse very slowly through cavities. Hence, the slope of the plot of −log(1-xt) and t0.65 increases rapidly at first and then slows down.

HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA with different concentrations of unloaded GA were incubated with VX2 cells for 24 h, with the cell viability still above 95 % (Fig. 6 a). This suggests that synthesized HMNPs-GQDs, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs, and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA have good biocompatibility and low toxicity. After incubation with VX2 cells for 24 h, HMNPs-GQDs(GA), HMNPs@PDA-GQDs(GA), and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA(GA) of different concentrations exhibited improved cell growth inhibition in vitro compared to the carriers of unloaded GA. As shown in Fig. 6b, the survival rate of VX2 cells incubated with HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA was 25.09 %, and that incubated with HMNPs@PDA-GQDs was 30.87 %. This is because FA has targeting properties, and more drug-loaded materials can reach the tumor to induce cell death. In addition, cell viability decreased with increasing concentration, indicating that the tumor inhibition activity of compound nanoparticles after drug loading are concentration dependent. Moreover, compared to HMNPs-GQDs(GA), HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA(GA) showed the highest lethality to VX2 cells, followed by HMNPs@PDA-GQDs(GA).

The cytotoxicity of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA to MCF-7 cells was also evaluated. The survival rate of VX2 cells incubated with HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA was lower than that of MCF-7 cells because of the overexpression of FA in VX2 cells (Fig. 6c and d). Owing to the low expression of folic acid in MCF-7 cells, HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA can enter VX2 cells more effectively and reduce cell survival rate, exhibiting greater anti-tumor effect in vivo.

The uptake of same amount of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA by cells was qualitatively investigated by confocal microscopy (Fig. 7 ). Both HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA could enter the cells and reach the nucleus within 24 h. The fluorescence intensity of VX2 cells incubated with HMNPs-GQDs@PDA-FA was higher than that of those incubated with HMNPs-GQDs@PDA. This is attributed to the higher cell absorption of composite nanoparticles grafted with FA.

Then, the effect of two vectors on VX2 tumor cells was studied in tumor-bearing mice. Fig. 8 a shows the volume changes in tumors after implantation for 28 days, with HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA having a higher tumor inhibitory effect (30.1 % of the initial tumor volume) than HMNPs@PDA-GQDs (47.8 %, Fig. 8a). Fig. 8b shows the HE staining of the tumor sections, with cells characterized by worm-like strips.

ce:section.ce:section-title: VX2 targeting ability in pulmonary vascular bed and liver
ce:section.ce:para: Using Na99TcmO4 eluent alone, CT images show that the Dark Grey shading of New Zealand rabbit head and neck glands, gastric mucosa, and bladder were very evident (Fig. 9 a). With HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-99Tcm, SPECT imaging shows the enhancement of Dark Grey shading in double kidney and bladder was very obvious, but SPECT failed to show the uptake of 99Tcm in the liver and lung (Fig. 9b). For HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA-99Tcm, SPECT imaging shows the Dark Grey shading of the liver and lung was obvious, and uptake of 99Tcm in the urinary bladder and kidney was noted (Fig. 9c). These results confirm the biological application of 99Tcm-labeled HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA and its accumulation in the pulmonary vascular bed and liver (endothelial network). Our results are in agreement with those of previous reports [29] highlighting the feasibility of porous magnetic drug carriers with FA as ligand. By using the specific interaction between the surface ligands of the nanodrug delivery system and the receptors expressed on target cells, the nanodrug delivery systems can be internalized by cancer cells by receptor-mediated endocytosis and release anticancer drugs to kill the cancer cells.

The preparation, drug loading and release, and toxicity of HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA were systematically investigated and demonstrated. HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA(GA) rapidly released GA within 24 h, with a continuous release for up to 190 h. The cumulative release of GA by HMNPs@PDA-GQDs and HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA was 33.39 % and 32.23 % at pH 7.4 and 50.13 % and 47.18 % at pH 5.7, respectively, indicating successful drug release in the acidic tumor microenvironment. Cytotoxicity analysis showed that the cell survival rate was ∼95 % in HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA without GA, dramatically decreasing to 25.09 % after loading GA. This indicates that HMNPs@PDA-GQDs-FA have negligible cytotoxic effects as a drug carrier system. Furthermore, the targeting performance of FA was demonstrated, leading to decreased tumor volume. Lastly, SPECT imaging analysis showed obvious accumulation in New Zealand rabbit liver and lung that was dependent on the conjugation of the FA ligand, the results highlight the feasibility of porous magnetic drug carriers with FA as ligand. These findings suggest the potential of composite nanomaterials in targeted cancer therapy.

Yubing Yang: Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Wenwen Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiangnong Liu: Investigation, Validation. Yuxiang Yang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 20577010and 20971043).
